The usage of this verb in the sense of "to pickle, to preserve" is due to the still widespread practice of pressing vegetables in brine to create a long-lasting product such as pickles and sauerkraut; see kbš #4 n.m.

We do not intend here to attempt to disambiguate clearly between Dt and Gt stems, but NB: even though the D stem with this verb is quite frequent, it is only in Mandaic where a distinction between Gt and Dt becomes clear.

DJBA distinguishes "ring" from this word on the basis of the Yemenite vocalization as if equivalent to the Hebrew word kippā and derives it then from the root kpp "to curve" and the meaning from the geonic explanation as Hebrew נזמים. Given the Syriac usage form precious stone "jewelry" and the fact that none of the citations indicate anything about rings rather than simply precious stones, we have not accepted his interpretation. A general semantic confusion with ˀbn is apparent only in Syriac, otherwise only when used as a material and large, natural stone objects.
Generally masculine in the targumim with the clear exception of Targum Proverbs under the influence of the Syriac usage.
For a very large list of the types of stones attested in Syriac see PSm-Thes. 1664ff.

See s.v. kln and cf. Greek κυκλάς ( Lehnw 287).
For Sam. see DJPA. In fact, though, the correct form is highly dubious; in the JLA tradition it is firmly established as kln with initial k_ preposition, and supposed Greek origins are quite dubious. Later targumim (as well as the vocalizations in JLAtg) appear to have misunderstood the initial k_ preposition in Onkelos as part of the noun, hence the double initial kaph. However, one could argue that in the Onkelos form an initial triple kaph has been simplified.

entries.form_section: BA constr.  <aram>kol </aram>,  <aram>k.Al+  </aram>, emph.   כֹּלָּא (i.e., with penultimate accent)
entries.end_notes:
We have not here attempted delineate how each dialect tends to go its own way as to morphosyntactic structures that are used (or not used!) to distinguish among "all x’s, all of x, every, each, the whole", etc.; for which we urge readers to consult the dialect grammars. Instead we have simply chosen to give examples of each type of construction attested for each dialect along with their general semantic force.
In general, though, as in other Semitic languages, kl with a singular definite noun means "the entire, the whole x" while "all x’s" may be expressed by using nouns in the singular absolute or plural definite states.

entries.end_notes: A raft made of inflated skins, the same as colloquial Arabic kelek. This word appears as a meaning of the old Sumerian loanword kalakku "storage container" (i.e. our klk #2) in Neo-Assyrian, but where the semantic development first occurred is not clear. entries.end_notes:
We are tempted to combine ksnyn 'little edible pieces of stuff' and ksnˀ 'coral' (see also s.v. ksytˀ) into a single lemma; indeed they were probably confused in antiquity. However it is probable that the former should be related to Akk. kasû, 'a common edible plant seed and spice', while the latter connected with Akk. kisittu, 'cutting, shaving' of tress and stones.
For the connection between coral and little 'trees' see Gk. lithodendron, Takahashi, BS BMM 220 and s.v. lytwdhndrwn.

Meaning 3 for BH נָא is not due to a misunderstanding of the Hebrew on the part of the targumists. It is rather a discourse marker as is "now" in English and other languages. Compare, for example, the substantial semantic and stress-position differences between "come nów!" and "cóme now".
It is quite possible that in some cases JlAtg מִכְעַן means "therefore" rather then "from now on".

entries.end_notes:
Primarily used as the introduction to paragraphs of letters.
The use in the Ezra passages was misunderstood in the Pesh traditions: Note that three different interpretations are given ("as it answered", a place name, and omitted), one for each exemplar! (Note, too, that the form וּכְעֶת of the MT at the end of Ezra 4:17 was clearly this form and so garbled by the Pesh.) The evidence is suggestive that the earliest text tradition may have known and used a similar word whose meaning was lost before the fixing of the tradition.

entries.end_notes: Greek κάρον? This word is the etymon of English caraway (from Latin from Arabic), but whether this is the precise aromatic seed originally meant by the Greek is not certain. The plant is native to western Asia, but the word seems variously to have also been used for "fennel" and "cumin" as well, both in antiquity and more recently in various languages. The latter is probably meant in the cited passage from LSt since of the three only cumin seems to have been regularly used externally for medicinal purposes. Flora 3:437f.

entries.form_section: Syr. and CPA also kwry; pl: PTA krwwN , Sam. krbN and (as if from kˁr#2) k)rh , LJLA kyrwwN , Syr. kr:awAtA), kr:ayA) , CPA kwr:y)
entries.end_notes:
From Akkadian karû (from Sum.) "grain pile" perhaps conflated with kurru "grain measure." LS2 separates ܟܘܪܝܐ and ܟܪܝܐ, taking the former from Greek κοριαῖος, but given the CPA spelling with w and that the Greek itself is almost certainly the same culture-word, that is a dubious distinction.
For the native lexicographic tradition as "grain storage" compare modern NENA bekāre < by n. + karyā, ‘lower part of house used for livestock and storage’, as pointed out by H. Mutzafi in "Akkadian substrate words and meanings surfacing in Neo-Aramaic", Brill’s Journal of Afroasiatic Languages and Linguistics 10 (2018):31.

entries.end_notes: The correct form of this noun is difficult to determine, both because of the massive variation in form (final -ין as a plural ending or as a part of the word? final /a/ vowel or /o/ vowel? second consonant ר or ד) and most importantly, whether the etymon is Greek καρχηδών "carbuncle" [so Lehnw 2:299] or χαλκηδών "chalcedony." [Both Greek forms are connected with Mediterranean geopgraphical terms, and hence it is unlikely that krkdn "rhinoceros" has anything to do with things, unless, perhaps, they were known only through Carthaginian contacts.] In any case it is quite possible that the forms with /d/ instead of /r/ are authentic dialectal variation, though scribal error cannot be ruled out.Löw in Lehnw 299.
Iranian; See Claudia A. Ciancaglini, "THE SYRIAC VERSION OF THE ALEXANDER ROMANCE", Le Muséon 114(2001):129.

The context there is probably always that of weapons of siege warfare thought to be among the strongest of substances. On the other hand, the apparently related (see Levy) LJLA word כרכמישא clearly means "lead".

entries.end_notes: Neither the actual spelling, the context, nor the the geonic interpretation support DJBA's identification of such a word as a Galilean pronunciation of "milk" with a laryngeal as opposed to a pharyngeal unvoiced fricative. The point of the story is that Galileans did not pronounce their guttural fricatives at all. Indeed, to judge by modern Aramaic, it is in the East where ḥeth was pronounced laryngeally, although there is some evidence for laryngeal pronunciation in the West in non-initial position; See AIOA s.v. kimahhu.

entries.end_notes: See discussion DJBA, although we assume otherwise; i.e., that it is in fact a gentilic based upon the Iranian word for "large grain jar" (see #1), especially given the context. The argument that the "jar maker" would have to be kndwqkr ignores the fact that the full Iranian form does not have to be borrowed; the Iranian "-kar" is often properly treated as a separate morpheme in Aramaic loans and can be added to native Aramaic words. So why can it not be omitted and replaced by an Aramaic gentilic ending?
